followupAnswerBank.question: How do you identify entities and relationships from a functional specification?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Functional specification se entities identify karne ke liye, main noun-verb analysis apply karta hu: nouns entities ya fields banti hain, verbs operations ya actions banti hain. Leave request FS ke liye, nouns Employee, Leave Request, Leave Type, Balance, Approval hain — ye entities ya reference tables pe map hote hain. Verbs submit, validate, approve, reject, calculate hain — ye RAP operations, validations, aur actions pe map hote hain. Nouns ke beech relationships CDS model mein associations define karte hain — ek employee ke kai leave requests hain matlab ek root-child composition ya HR data ka association.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: To identify entities from a functional specification, I apply noun-verb analysis: nouns become entities or fields, verbs become operations or actions. For the leave request FS, nouns are Employee, Leave Request, Leave Type, Balance, Approval — these map to entities or reference tables. Verbs are submit, validate, approve, reject, calculate — these map to RAP operations, validations, and actions. Relationships between nouns define associations in the CDS model — one employee has many leave requests means a root-child composition or an association to HR data.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: FS mein ek complex sentence tha: 'Travel request includes multiple expense items, each with category, amount, and receipt.' Maine identify kiya: Travel Request = root entity, Expense Items = child entity (composition). Category = value list. Amount = decimal field. Receipt = attachment entity. Teen entities, ek composition — CDS model clear ho gaya.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Ek FS sentence se multiple entities miss karna — har noun carefully check karo. Sometimes entities ek sentence mein implied hote hain: 'manager approval with date and comments' — ApprovedBy, ApprovedAt, ApprovalComment fields implied hain.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Noun-Verb Analysis of FS:
" FS Text:
" 'Employee submits leave request specifying leave type,
"  start and end dates. System calculates working days.
"  Manager reviews and approves or rejects with comment.'

" NOUNS → Entities/Fields:
" Employee      → EmployeeId (field, FK to HR)
" Leave Request → ZRAP_LeaveRequest (root CDS entity)
" Leave Type    → LeaveType (field + value list)
" Start Date    → StartDate (field, mandatory)
" End Date      → EndDate (field, mandatory)
" Working Days  → LeaveDays (field, calculated)
" Manager       → ApprovedBy (field, set on action)
" Comment       → RejectReason (field, action param)

" VERBS → Operations/Actions:
" submits       → create operation in BDEF
" calculates    → determination: calcLeaveDays
" approves      → action: approve
" rejects       → action: reject

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you convert business validations from FS to RAP technical validations?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: FS validation ko RAP technical validation mein convert karne ke liye, main chhe elements extract karta hu: validation name (descriptive), trigger — final submission checks ke liye on save ya interactive feedback ke liye on modify, trigger fields — kaunse field changes validation fire karne chahiye, business rule — pseudocode mein exact logic, dynamic values ke liye placeholders ke saath error message text, aur field reference taaki Fiori problematic field highlight kar sake. Har FS rule jaise 'start date future mein honi chahiye' BDEF mein validation definition aur implementation class mein corresponding FOR VALIDATE method banti hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: To convert an FS validation to a RAP technical validation, I extract six elements: validation name (descriptive), trigger — on save for final submission checks or on modify for interactive feedback, trigger fields — which field changes should fire the validation, the business rule — exact logic in pseudocode, the error message text with placeholders for dynamic values, and the field reference so Fiori can highlight the problematic field. Each FS rule like 'start date must be in the future' becomes a validation definition in BDEF and a corresponding FOR VALIDATE method in the implementation class.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: FS mein tha: 'Leave request cannot overlap with existing approved leaves.' TS mein banaya: validateNoOverlap, on save, trigger fields StartDate EndDate, logic: SELECT from ZLEAVE_REQUEST where EmployeeId = current AND Status = 'APPROVED' AND date range overlaps. Error message: 'Leave dates overlap with approved request {ID}.' — dynamic ID included. Implementation: READ ENTITIES plus SELECT, REPORTED with message.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: FS validation ko literally copy-paste as a comment in ABAP — validation must be properly coded. Also: on modify vs on save decision matters. Agar user ko real-time feedback chahiye as they type — on modify. Agar only at submit time — on save. FS often doesn't specify this — ask or decide based on UX.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" FS Validation → TS Specification:
" FS: 'End date must be after start date'
" TS Validation Spec:
"   Name:    validateDateRange
"   Trigger: on save
"   Fields:  StartDate, EndDate
"   Rule:    EndDate >= StartDate
"   Message: 'End date must be on or after start date'
"   Field ref: EndDate (highlighted in Fiori)

" BDEF Entry:
validation validateDateRange on save { field StartDate, EndDate; }

" Implementation:
METHOD validateDateRange FOR VALIDATE
    IMPORTING keys FOR LeaveRequest~validateDateRange.
  READ ENTITIES OF ZRAP_LeaveRequest
    ENTITY LeaveRequest FIELDS ( StartDate EndDate )
    WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys ) RESULT DATA(lt_lr).
  LOOP AT lt_lr INTO DATA(ls).
    IF ls-EndDate < ls-StartDate.
      APPEND VALUE #( %key = ls-%key ) TO failed-leaverequest.
      APPEND VALUE #( %key = ls-%key
        %element-EndDate = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
        %msg = new_message_with_text(
          severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
          text = 'End date must be on or after start date' ) )
        TO reported-leaverequest.
    ENDIF.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you map FS screen requirements to Fiori Elements floor plans?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Main FS screen requirements ko Fiori Elements floor plans pe map karta hu har screen ke purpose ko match karke: filtering aur sorting ke saath records ki list List Report floor plan pe map hoti hai. Editable fields aur action buttons ke saath ek single record ka detail view Object Page pe map hota hai. KPI tiles aur charts ke saath analytical overview Analytical List Page pe map hota hai. Guided multi-step entry form sections ke saath wizard-style Object Page pe map hota hai, ya custom SAPUI5 agar flow complex hai. Standard business screens almost always List Report plus Object Page combination pe map hote hain — jo most common Fiori Elements pattern hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: I map FS screen requirements to Fiori Elements floor plans by matching the purpose of each screen: a list of records with filtering and sorting maps to List Report floor plan. A detail view of a single record with editable fields and action buttons maps to Object Page. An analytical overview with KPI tiles and charts maps to Analytical List Page. A guided multi-step entry form maps to a wizard-style Object Page with sections, or custom SAPUI5 if the flow is complex. Standard business screens almost always map to List Report plus Object Page combination — which is the most common Fiori Elements pattern.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Leave request FS had three screens: employee dashboard showing my requests, manager queue showing pending approvals, and detail screen with approve/reject. Employee dashboard → List Report filtered by EmployeeId. Manager queue → List Report filtered by Status = PENDING. Detail screen → Object Page with approve/reject buttons. All three: Fiori Elements, zero custom SAPUI5. FS to UI mapping took 30 minutes — implementation took 2 days.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Har screen ke liye custom SAPUI5 assume karna — most transactional screens fit standard Fiori floor plans. Only after confirming a floor plan cannot meet the requirement should custom development begin.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you estimate effort from a functional specification for a RAP project?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: FS se RAP project ke liye effort estimate karne ke liye, main technical components count karta hu: CDS entities ki number data model effort determine karti hai, validations aur determinations ki number behavior implementation effort determine karti hai, actions ki number workflow implementation effort determine karti hai, aur screens ki number Fiori annotation effort determine karti hai. Main component-based estimation use karta hu: har CDS entity unit test ke saath average 0.5 se 1 din, har validation 0.5 se 1 din, har action complexity ke depend pe 0.5 se 2 din, har Fiori Elements screen annotations ke saath 1 se 2 din. Main integration complexity aur unknowns ke liye 20% buffer add karta hu, plus transport aur release activities ke liye 1 din.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: To estimate effort from an FS for a RAP project, I count the technical components: number of CDS entities determines the data model effort, number of validations and determinations determines behavior implementation effort, number of actions determines workflow implementation effort, and number of screens determines Fiori annotation effort. I use component-based estimation: each CDS entity averages 0.5 to 1 day including unit test, each validation 0.5 to 1 day, each action 0.5 to 2 days depending on complexity, each Fiori Elements screen 1 to 2 days including annotations. I add 20% buffer for integration complexity and unknowns, plus 1 day for transport and release activities.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Leave request FS: 1 root entity, 1 child entity (attachments), 3 validations, 1 determination, 2 actions, 2 Fiori screens. Estimate: 2 entities (1 day), 3 validations (1.5 days), 1 determination (0.5 day), 2 actions (1 day), 2 screens (3 days) = 7 days. +20% buffer = 8.4 → 9 days. +1 day transport = 10 days. Actual delivery: 11 days. Estimate was 90% accurate — buffer absorbed one unexpected integration requirement.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: FS mein implicit requirements count nahi karna — 'leave balance check' ke peeche RFC to HR system hai — ye extra effort hai. Always ask: external system calls hain? Complex calculations hain? Authentication requirements special hain? These add effort.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Effort Estimation Template — RAP Project
" Component              Count  Estimate/unit  Total
" CDS Root Entities        1    1.0 day        1.0 day
" CDS Child Entities       1    0.5 day        0.5 day
" Validations              3    0.75 day avg   2.25 days
"   validateDateRange           0.5 day
"   validateBalance             1.0 day (RFC call)
"   validateNoOverlap           0.75 day
" Determinations           1    0.5 day        0.5 day
" Actions                  2    0.75 day avg   1.5 days
"   approve action              0.5 day
"   reject action (param)       1.0 day
" Fiori Screens            2    1.5 day avg    3.0 days
"   List Report                 1.0 day
"   Object Page                 2.0 days
" Unit Tests               -    20% overhead   1.75 days
" Transport + Release      -    fixed          1.0 day
" ----------------------------------------   11.5 days
" Buffer 15%                                   1.7 days
" TOTAL ESTIMATE                              13 days

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you handle requirements that do not fit standard RAP patterns?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Jab requirement standard RAP patterns mein fit nahi kare, main alternatives ko order mein assess karta hu: existing BAPI logic wrap karne ke liye unmanaged RAP — RAP layer OData V4 interface provide karta hai jabki FOR MODIFY existing BAPI call karta hai. Fiori se triggered complex batch processing ke liye, ek RAP action jo JES ya SM36 API ke through ABAP background job trigger karta hai. Non-standard UI requirements ke liye, custom SAPUI5 jo same RAP OData V4 service consume karta hai. Main har standard RAP se deviation ko technical specification mein business justification aur chosen alternative approach ke saath document karta hu.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: When a requirement does not fit standard RAP patterns, I assess the alternatives in order: unmanaged RAP for wrapping existing BAPI logic — the RAP layer provides the OData V4 interface while FOR MODIFY calls the existing BAPI. For complex batch processing triggered from Fiori, a RAP action that triggers an ABAP background job via JES or SM36 API. For non-standard UI requirements, custom SAPUI5 that consumes the same RAP OData V4 service. I document every deviation from standard RAP in the technical specification with the business justification and the chosen alternative approach.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Ek client requirement tha: 'Bulk upload leave requests from Excel.' Standard RAP nahi hota — no native Excel upload. Solution: RAP action 'bulkUpload' that accepts a file reference, a separate ABAP report processes the file and calls MODIFY ENTITIES in batches, results returned via a custom BAPI-like response. Hybrid — RAP for single request CRUD, custom report for bulk, same Fiori UI for both. TS mein clearly documented tha 'bulk upload is custom, not standard RAP.'
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Trying to force every requirement into standard RAP even when it genuinely doesn't fit — creates overly complex behavior implementations. Pragmatic hybrid approach is often the right answer. Document the choice, don't hide it.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Non-Standard Requirement: Bulk Leave Upload
" FS: 'HR should be able to upload Excel with 100+ leave requests'

" TS Decision: Hybrid approach
" Option 1 (Pure RAP): Not feasible — no Excel parsing in RAP
" Option 2 (Custom + RAP):
"   Step 1: Fiori uploads Excel to AL11 temp folder
"          (using Standard Upload feature or SAPUI5 FileUploader)
"   Step 2: RAP action 'processBulkUpload' triggers
"          ABAP background job via JES API
"   Step 3: Background job reads Excel (via custom FM)
"          validates each row, calls:
           MODIFY ENTITIES OF ZRAP_LeaveRequest
             ENTITY LeaveRequest CREATE
             WITH lt_bulk_requests
             MAPPED DATA(mapped)
             FAILED DATA(failed)
             REPORTED DATA(reported).
"   Step 4: Results returned via custom status table
"          User can check job status from Fiori
" TS Note: 'Bulk upload uses hybrid pattern — standard RAP
"          insufficient for batch Excel processing.'

followupAnswerBank.question: What questions do you ask the business analyst when FS is unclear for RAP development?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Jab FS unclear hai, main technical specification likhne se pehle ambiguity remove karne ke liye targeted questions puchta hu. Key questions: complete status lifecycle values kya hain aur kaunse transitions allowed hain — ye actions aur validations define karta hai. Kaunse fields truly mandatory hain vs optional — ye BDEF field controls define karta hai. Kya validations real-time feedback dene chahiye jab user type kare ya sirf submission pe — ye on-modify vs on-save trigger determine karta hai. Approval single-level hai ya multi-level, aur kya SLA-based escalations hain — ye determine karta hai ki RAP actions kaafi hain ya workflow integration chahiye. Leave balance data kahan se aata hai — kya SAP HR mein hai, custom table, ya external system — ye RFC dependency determine karta hai aur effort significantly impact karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: When an FS is unclear, I ask targeted questions to remove ambiguity before writing the technical specification. Key questions: What are the complete status lifecycle values and which transitions are allowed — this defines actions and validations. Which fields are truly mandatory versus optional — this defines BDEF field controls. Should validations give real-time feedback as the user types or only at submission — this determines on-modify versus on-save trigger. Is approval single-level or multi-level, and are there SLA-based escalations — this determines if RAP actions are sufficient or if workflow integration is needed. Where does leave balance data come from — is it in SAP HR, a custom table, or an external system — this determines RFC dependency and impacts effort significantly.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: FS mein tha: 'System should check leave eligibility.' Interview-style analysis: BA se pucha: 'Eligibility kahan stored hai? HR infotype IT2006 mein? Ya custom table mein? Real-time RFC call ya cached data?' Answer: HR IT2006, real-time call. Technical impact: RFC call in validation — latency risk, timeout handling needed. Effort +0.5 days. Had I not asked, I would have estimated wrong and potentially built a slower solution.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Assuming FS is complete and jumping to coding — FS always has gaps. Questions upfront save rework later. Document every clarification as a formal FS amendment or meeting minutes with BA sign-off.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" FS Clarification Checklist — Questions to Ask BA:
" Before writing TS, get answers to:

" 1. Status Lifecycle:
"    Q: What status values exist?
"    Q: Which transitions are allowed? (DRAFT→SUBMITTED→APPROVED)
"    Q: Can APPROVED be reverted? Under what conditions?

" 2. Field Rules:
"    Q: List of mandatory vs optional fields?
"    Q: Any conditional mandatory (if LeaveType=SICK then
"       Doctor certificate mandatory)?

" 3. Validation Timing:
"    Q: Should date validation fire as user picks dates
"       (real-time) or only at submit?

" 4. Approval:
"    Q: One manager approves, or multiple levels?
"    Q: If manager inactive, who is substitute?

" 5. External Data:
"    Q: Leave balance source — HR infotype or custom table?
"    Q: Org structure for manager determination — HR PA or
"       custom table?

" Document answers → Update FS → Sign off → Write TS

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you write the data model section of a technical specification for RAP?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: RAP technical specification ke data model section mein shaamil hain: database table name saare fields ke saath, unke data types, key field designation, mandatory field markers, aur optimistic locking ke liye system timestamp fields. Draft-enabled objects ke liye draft table name. CDS entity design — root entity name, child entity names, aur unke beech association definitions. Leave type ya status jaise dropdown fields ke liye value list entities. Status fields ke liye domain definitions valid values dikhate hue. Ye section itna detailed hona chahiye ki developer additional clarification ki zarurat ke bina SE11 mein table aur ADT mein CDS views bana sake.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: The data model section of a RAP technical specification includes: the database table name with all fields, their data types, key field designation, mandatory field markers, and system timestamp fields for optimistic locking. The draft table name for draft-enabled objects. The CDS entity design — root entity name, child entity names, and association definitions between them. Value list entities for dropdown fields like leave type or status. Domain definitions for status fields showing valid values. This section should be detailed enough that a developer can create the table in SE11 and the CDS views in ADT without needing additional clarification.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: TS banate time ek table tha: ZLEAVE_REQUEST. Documentation mein clearly likha: key field MANDT + REQUEST_ID (GUID), mandatory fields, CREATED_BY, CREATED_AT, CHANGED_BY, CHANGED_AT (for etag), STATUS as domain ZLEAVESTATUS with values: DRAFT, SUBMITTED, APPROVED, REJECTED. Draft table: ZDT_LEAVEREQ. Developer ne exact spec se table banaya — zero rework.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Data type precision miss karna — amounts ko DEC(15,2) likhna better hai sirf 'decimal' likhne se. Timestamp fields miss karna — without CHANGED_AT, optimistic locking (etag) BDEF mein define nahi hoga correctly.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" TS Data Model Section — ZLEAVE_REQUEST Table:
" Field         | Type           | Len | Key | Mandatory | Note
" -------------|----------------|-----|-----|-----------|------
" MANDT        | CLNT           | 3   | YES | YES       | Client
" REQUEST_ID   | CHAR (GUID)    | 22  | YES | YES       | Key
" EMPLOYEE_ID  | NUMC           | 8   |     | YES       | FK PA0000
" LEAVE_TYPE   | CHAR           | 4   |     | YES       | Domain ZLEAVETYP
" START_DATE   | DATS           | 8   |     | YES       |
" END_DATE     | DATS           | 8   |     | YES       |
" LEAVE_DAYS   | DEC            | 5,2 |     | NO        | Calculated

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you review a technical specification before development starts?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: TS review ko checklist use karke FS ke against completeness verify karna chahiye: har FS requirement kam se kam ek TS component se traced ho — koi requirement undocumented na ho. Data model saare fields ko correct types aur keys ke saath cover kare. BDEF section saare operations, trigger conditions aur field references ke saath validations, trigger fields ke saath determinations, aur parameters ke saath actions cover kare. Har validation ke liye error messages exact text ke saath defined hon. Authorization roles aur objects listed hon. Transport sequence documented ho. Fiori floor plan aur key annotations planned hon. Main jo developer implement karega aur jo BA ne FS likha hai dono ko review mein involve karta hu — developer technical completeness check karta hai, BA business completeness check karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: A TS review should verify completeness against the FS using a checklist: every FS requirement is traced to at least one TS component — no requirement is undocumented. The data model covers all fields with correct types and keys. The BDEF section covers all operations, validations with trigger conditions and field references, determinations with trigger fields, and actions with parameters. Error messages are defined with exact text for each validation. Authorization roles and objects are listed. Transport sequence is documented. Fiori floor plan and key annotations are planned. I involve both the developer who will implement and the BA who wrote the FS in the review — the developer checks technical completeness, the BA checks business completeness.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: TS review mein ek important gap mila: validation 'validateBalance' ka error message defined nahi tha. Developer ne generic message daala — 'Insufficient balance.' Business ne actually wanted: 'Your available {LeaveType} balance is {X} days. You requested {Y} days. Reduce your request or contact HR.' Ye difference TS review ne pakda — production mein user confusion se bacha.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: TS ko sirf developer review karna — business logic gaps developer nahi pakad paata. BA review ensures business correctness; developer review ensures technical feasibility and completeness.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" TS Review Checklist:
" FS Traceability:
" [ ] Every FS requirement has TS entry
" [ ] FS acceptance criteria map to validation/test cases

" Data Model:
" [ ] All fields with types and lengths
" [ ] Key field(s) defined
" [ ] Mandatory fields marked
" [ ] System fields (CreatedAt, ChangedAt) for etag
" [ ] Draft table defined (if draft needed)

" Behavior:
" [ ] All BDEF operations listed
" [ ] Field controls (mandatory, readonly) complete
" [ ] Each validation: name, trigger, fields, rule, message
" [ ] Each determination: name, trigger, fields, logic
" [ ] Each action: name, parameters, status change, side effects

" UI:
" [ ] Floor plan specified (List Report, Object Page)
" [ ] Key annotations planned (@UI.lineItem, @UI.fieldGroup)
" [ ] Action buttons planned (which Object Page section)

" Non-Functional:
" [ ] Authorization: roles and auth objects
" [ ] Transport sequence documented
" [ ] Performance: large data sets handled?
